q3: The model kept defaulting to sender-centered framing in the stakes section — it took several rounds to get the 'why this matters' to land from the reader's side instead of ours. The action item also kept splitting into menus (two contact methods, two things the contact person could do) even after I flagged it.
q5: I glanced at it. 'Reader-Centered Framing' and 'Actionable Precision' are basically what I kept pushing for, so yes, it captured the right things. But having the rubric visible didn't seem to prevent the model from repeating the same menu-of-options mistake multiple times — so it helped in theory more than in practice.
q6: It made me notice that the model was consistent about fixing one thing at a time rather than internalizing the pattern. Like, it would nail the stakes framing but then immediately produce a split action item. The rubric framed these as separate criteria, which might actually be why — the model treated them as independent checkboxes instead of a unified feel.

q3: The model kept centering consequences on us — 'we'll move to a new provider,' 'we'd like to hear it' — instead of making the reader feel the weight of their own situation. It took several rounds of me naming the exact problem before it clicked that 'we will do X' and 'this account goes to a competitor' are structurally different, not just stylistically different.
q5: I looked at it, and it's closer than I expected — 'Reader-Centered Framing' and 'Structural Momentum' map reasonably well to what I actually care about. But the rubric describes the pattern without capturing the feel of why it matters, so the model could technically satisfy the criteria while still missing the point, which is what happened in earlier rounds.
q6: The rubric made me notice that the model was consistently good at structure and consistently slow on framing — it would get the order right but default to sender-perspective language inside each section. Without the rubric naming 'reader or thing-at-stake as subject,' I probably would have just called it a tone problem and not pinpointed where the subject-verb choice was doing the damage.

q3: The model kept defaulting to closing lines that trailed off into sentiment or acknowledgment — 'thanks for advocating for this one' is the clearest example. It took a few rounds to get it to understand that the close should be forward-looking and action-oriented, not warm and trailing. The middle framing was also off early — it was narrating the situation rather than naming what the reader was actually walking into.
q5: I did look at it. The rubric captured a lot of what I care about — especially 'Structural Momentum' and 'Reader-Centered Framing,' those map pretty well to how I think about email flow. Where it felt a little abstract was 'Functional Sentences' — that criterion is right but it's the kind of thing that's hard to operationalize without examples, and I'm not sure the model always knew which sentences were doing redundant work versus necessary work.
q6: Seeing the rubric made me realize the model was pretty good at fixing individual problems when I pointed them out, but it wasn't anticipating them — it wasn't reading the draft holistically the way a strong editor would. The rubric might have helped it get there faster if it had been applied more proactively rather than reactively, because I kept having to surface things the rubric already named.
